The surge is coming fast and I need to get my nets off the beach before the tide rips them away.
My boat is tossing like a cork in this gale and I pray the moorings hold until the storm passes.
I can see the water rising over the pier and I fear for the gear I left in the shed.
The sky has turned a bruised purple and the fish have all gone deep to hide from this chaos.
I have to secure the hatches and double-knot the lines or the wind will tear this vessel apart.
The tide is pulling out far too fast while that green light splits the sky wide open.
My nets are shivering in the water and the fish have all gone deep and silent.
I have never seen the stars dance like that while the wind dies down to nothing.
That shimmering rift in the clouds is making the surface of the bay glow like silver.
I should pull the lines in before whatever is happening up there turns the sea against me.
You can't just push your markers past the reef where the snapper congregate.
My nets have been cast in these waters since my father first taught me.
That cove is the only place the tide pulls the shrimp right into my traps.
If you block the channel, I can't get my boat out before the storm hits.
We need a clear line so I don't tangle my gear in your illegal pots.
I don't care what kind of magic this is, but it's spooking every fish in the bay.
If I cast my line through that shimmering hole, will the hook even find a bottom?
This glowing rift is churning the water up and making the tide run completely backwards.
I've spent forty years on these waters and never seen a current that pulls from thin air.
My nets are getting tangled in a light that doesn't cast a shadow on the deck.
I need to find a clearing in these thickets to see if there is any running water nearby.
My boots are getting snagged on every root, and I hope my tackle box isn't scraping against these rocks.
If I can just push through this brush, I might find a stream where the trout are hiding.
These dense vines are making it nearly impossible to carry my rods without snapping a tip.
I can smell the dampness of a riverbank somewhere ahead, but the foliage is blocking my path.
The tide has gone out and not a single ripple has touched my line for hours.
I can hear nothing but the slap of water against the hull while I wait for a bite.
My shoulders are stiff from sitting still in this fog with no sign of a school nearby.
The wind has died down completely and the lake is as silent as a graveyard today.
I will stay out here in the stillness until the fish decide to wake up and feed.
You best back off before I wrap this heavy gauge net around your neck.
I've hauled bigger monsters than you out of the deep with nothing but a line.
Step away from my boat or you'll find out how sharp this filleting knife is.
You're making a big mistake threatening a man who spends his life fighting the tide.
Keep talking and I'll make sure you end up as bait for the crabs.
This hand-carved cedar lure has brought me more trophy bass than any store-bought plug ever could.
I keep this old graphite rod polished because it's the only one that doesn't snap under a heavy haul.
My grandfather gave me this brass compass, and it has guided me through every fog on the coast.
This heavy-duty waterproof chest keeps my best flies dry even when the swells are crashing over the bow.
There is no finer piece of gear than this saltwater reel that never jams when a marlin hits.
If I take too many salmon from this stream today, there won't be enough spawners left for next year.
The nets are coming up lighter every morning, and I fear the cove is finally running dry.
I have to move my traps further offshore because the coastal waters are stripped completely bare.
We must agree on a limit for the daily catch or we will all starve by winter.
It hurts to throw these small ones back, but they are the only hope for the future of the reef.
